channel.title: Math Learning Site – Worksheets
channel.description: Recent content in Worksheets on Math Learning Site

item.title: Solve by Factoring Worksheet
item.description:
This solve by factoring worksheet helps students practice factoring quadratic equations and applying the zero product property. The printable set includes 12 randomized questions and an answer key on page 2.

What is Solving by Factoring?

Solving by factoring means rewriting a quadratic equation as a product of two factors equal to zero. Once the equation is factored, you set each factor equal to zero and solve the simpler equations.

How to Solve by Factoring

  1. Move all terms to one side so the equation equals zero.
  2. Factor the quadratic expression completely.
  3. Set each factor equal to zero.
  4. Solve each equation to get the roots.

Example

Solve x2+5x+6=0x^2 + 5x + 6 = 0x2+5x+6=0

Factor: (x+2)(x+3)=0(x + 2)(x + 3) = 0(x+2)(x+3)=0

Set each factor equal to zero:

x+2=0x + 2 = 0x+2=0 or x+3=0x + 3 = 0x+3=0

So the solutions are x=−2x = -2x=−2 and x=−3x = -3x=−3.

Solve by Factoring Worksheet

12 questions Page 1 of 2

Solve each problem. Show your work.

Name:
Date:

Solve by Factoring Worksheet Answer Key

12 questions Page 2 of 2

Check your work using the correct answers below.

FAQ

Q: When should I use factoring to solve a quadratic?
A: Factoring works best when the quadratic expression can be broken into integer or simple rational factors.

Q: Why do we set each factor equal to zero?
A: The zero product property says that if two factors multiply to zero, then at least one of the factors must be zero.

item.title: Quadratic Formula Worksheet
item.description:
This quadratic formula worksheet gives students practice identifying coefficients, substituting carefully, and simplifying answers correctly. The printable set includes 12 randomized questions and an answer key on page 2.

What is the Quadratic Formula?

The quadratic formula is a method for solving any quadratic equation written in the form ax2+bx+c=0ax^2 + bx + c = 0ax2+bx+c=0. It is especially useful when a quadratic is hard to factor.

How to Use the Quadratic Formula

  1. Rewrite the equation in standard form: ax2+bx+c=0ax^2 + bx + c = 0ax2+bx+c=0.
  2. Identify the values of aaa, bbb, and ccc.
  3. Substitute into the quadratic formula.
  4. Simplify carefully to find the solution or solutions.

Example

Solve x2−4x−5=0x^2 - 4x - 5 = 0x2−4x−5=0

Here, a=1a = 1a=1, b=−4b = -4b=−4, and c=−5c = -5c=−5.

Substitute into the formula:

x=−(−4)±(−4)2−4(1)(−5)2(1)x = \frac{-(-4) \pm \sqrt{(-4)^2 - 4(1)(-5)}}{2(1)}x=2(1)−(−4)±(−4)2−4(1)(−5)​​

x=4±16+202=4±62x = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 + 20}}{2} = \frac{4 \pm 6}{2}x=24±16+20​​=24±6​

So the solutions are x=5x = 5x=5 and x=−1x = -1x=−1.

Quadratic Formula Worksheet

12 questions Page 1 of 2

Solve each problem. Show your work.

Name:
Date:

Quadratic Formula Worksheet Answer Key

12 questions Page 2 of 2

Check your work using the correct answers below.

FAQ

Q: Can the quadratic formula solve every quadratic equation?
A: Yes. As long as the equation is quadratic and written in standard form, the quadratic formula can be used.

Q: What does the discriminant tell me?
A: The discriminant, b2−4acb^2 - 4acb2−4ac, tells you whether the equation has two real solutions, one real solution, or no real solutions.

item.title: Completing the Square Worksheet
item.description:
This completing the square worksheet helps students practice turning quadratic expressions into perfect-square trinomials and solving from there. The printable set includes 12 randomized questions and an answer key on page 2.

What is Completing the Square?

Completing the square is a method for rewriting a quadratic expression so that it becomes a perfect square plus or minus a constant. This makes some quadratic equations easier to solve and helps connect equations to vertex form.

How to Complete the Square

  1. Move the constant term to the other side.
  2. Take half of the coefficient of xxx.
  3. Square that number.
  4. Add it to both sides of the equation.
  5. Rewrite the left side as a square and solve.

Example

Solve x2+6x−7=0x^2 + 6x - 7 = 0x2+6x−7=0

Move the constant:

x2+6x=7x^2 + 6x = 7x2+6x=7

Take half of 666, which is 333, and square it to get 999.

Add 999 to both sides:

x2+6x+9=16x^2 + 6x + 9 = 16x2+6x+9=16

Rewrite:

(x+3)2=16(x + 3)^2 = 16(x+3)2=16

So x+3=±4x + 3 = \pm 4x+3=±4, which gives x=1x = 1x=1 or x=−7x = -7x=−7.

Completing the Square Worksheet

12 questions Page 1 of 2

Solve each problem. Show your work.

Name:
Date:

Completing the Square Worksheet Answer Key

12 questions Page 2 of 2

Check your work using the correct answers below.

FAQ

Q: Why do we add the same number to both sides?
A: Adding the same number to both sides keeps the equation balanced.

Q: When is completing the square useful?
A: It is especially helpful when factoring is difficult and when rewriting a quadratic into vertex form.

item.title: Mixed Quadratic Equations Worksheet
item.description:
This mixed quadratic equations worksheet combines factoring, the quadratic formula, and completing the square in one printable review. It includes 16 randomized questions and an answer key on page 2.

What Does This Worksheet Cover?

This worksheet is designed for mixed practice across the three major methods for solving quadratic equations. Students can review when to factor, when to use the quadratic formula, and when completing the square is the clearest method.

How to Approach Mixed Quadratic Problems

  1. Check whether the equation can be factored easily.
  2. If not, decide whether the quadratic formula or completing the square is more efficient.
  3. Solve carefully and check your results when possible.

Example

Solve x2−9=0x^2 - 9 = 0x2−9=0

This factors as (x−3)(x+3)=0(x - 3)(x + 3) = 0(x−3)(x+3)=0.

So the solutions are x=3x = 3x=3 and x=−3x = -3x=−3.

Mixed Quadratic Equations Worksheet

16 questions Page 1 of 2

Solve each problem. Show your work.

Name:
Date:

Mixed Quadratic Equations Worksheet Answer Key

16 questions Page 2 of 2

Check your work using the correct answers below.

FAQ

Q: Do I have to use the same method for every problem?
A: No. Mixed quadratic practice is about choosing the method that fits the equation best.

Q: What if more than one method works?
A: That is normal. Some quadratic equations can be solved using more than one method, but one method is often faster or cleaner than the others.
